content:
3 min read
6 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

A few weeks ago I shared FrameUI, an Angular component library I’ve been building. The first version was heavily focused on the visual direction: sharp edges, visible structure, corner handles, and a more technical look than many of the softer UI libraries out there.
One piece of feedback stuck with me: Angular already has libraries that feel close to shadcn/ui. If FrameUI should exist, it should not try to become another one of those. That was fair.
So for 0.5.0, I did not try to make FrameUI more generic by default. The default style is still sharp. The technical style is still the starting point.
But I also wanted to make it easier to adjust FrameUI without fighting every component individually.
The main change in 0.5.0 is that radius, density, and shadows now work through shared settings across the library. Before this, some values were spread around too much. That made customization possible in theory, but not always reliable in practice.
Now you can configure things like:

• Light or dark mode

• Main color

• Background, text, surface, border, and focus colors

• Status colors

• Radius in a few fixed steps

• Density in a few fixed steps

• Shadow level in a few fixed steps
The goal is not to turn FrameUI into a full theme builder right now. It is more about making the existing design easier to adapt while keeping the library consistent.
I also added a “Customize Preview” menu to the docs header. It lets you quickly try different palettes, radius settings, density settings, shadows, and corner handles directly in the docs.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Want something more vibrant and round? Try Plasma Violet and increase the radius.
Want something subtle, but you do not like the rough edges? Keep the palette calm and change the radius to fit your app.
Satisfied with the settings? Easily export it.
Some component details were cleaned up as part of this release too. Buttons, tooltips, radius behavior, density handling, and shadows were adjusted so they follow the same base settings more reliably.
This release ended up being bigger than I first expected. I originally planned it as a smaller 0.4.3 update, but the changes touched enough of the foundation that 0.5.0 made more sense.
The important part for me is this: FrameUI should keep its own look by default, but users should not be forced into every detail of that look.
That is the direction I want to continue with. Not unlimited styling freedom everywhere, but enough control to make the library fit into real Angular apps without losing what makes it recognizable.
Docs: https://frame-ui.com
GitHub: https://github.com/gamekohl/frame-ui
